explanation_used:
1. **Opening statement**: This neuron strongly responds to **text containing the directional term "northeast" or its direct translations and common abbreviations** (such as "northeastern," "north-east," "noreste," or "nord-est").

2. **Domain coverage**: The pattern appears across multiple languages (English, Spanish, Romanian, Norwegian) and in diverse contexts: geographic reports (earthquakes, cyclones, constituencies), institutional names ("Northeastern Professor"), regional descriptions ("northeastern states"), and even in corporate or index names ("Morgan Stanley Technology Index" where "Technology Index" abbreviates to "T Index," mimicking the "NE" structure). The neuron also activates for partial or adjacent strings like "norte" or "nord" when followed by a hyphen or space and an "e" sound.

3. **Contrast with low-activation examples**: The low-activation texts completely lack any reference to the compound directional term "northeast" or its linguistic equivalents. They cover unrelated topics like celebrity news, sports, general tech policy, and local events with no geographic directional markers. The key distinguishing element is the presence of the specific directional phrase.

4. **Closing interpretation**: This neuron functions as a highly specific detector for the lexical token "northeast" and its cross-linguistic variants. It appears to key on the character sequence and phonetic concept rather than a high-level geographic theme, as it fires even in non-geographic contexts like art installations or financial indices where the string appears.

results.actual_text: the conservative-leaning Wisconsin Institute of Law & Liberty, filed a free speech lawsuit against Northeast Wisconsin Technical College. The case in the U.S. Eastern District of Wisconsin is
results.plain_text: Waving a homemade valentine, Green Bay's Polly Olsen was among several student free speech advocates who appeared during a White House signing ceremony Thursday in Washington, D.C. President Donald Trump signed an executive order to promote free speech on campus, a mostly symbolic measure welcomed by his conservative base. In 2018, Olsen, represented by the conservative-leaning Wisconsin Institute of Law & Liberty, filed a free speech lawsuit against Northeast Wisconsin Technical College. The case in the U.S. Eastern District of Wisconsin is ongoing. Olsen sought to hand out homemade valentines with Bible verses on Valentine's Day on her college campus. She was stopped by campus security. "College officials stopped her and told her that she would be restricted to so-called free speech zones because some people might find her cards offensive," Trump said. "I don't. In fact, Polly, give me some, I'll send them around." Olsen said "freedom of speech is near and dear to my heart," adding her mother told her that she would need to know her First Amendment rights "because someday they would be violated and I would have to stand up for them." Olsen said she is carrying on her mother's legacy by handing out "little valentines, encouraging people to know that they are loved and cared for." Olsen said she's "just one of many students that are out there that universities and schools are trying to shut down, sweep it under the rug and make them be quiet." She said "without freedom of speech, we don't have America anymore." Karen Smits, vice president of college advancement at NWTC, said "the lawsuit was never about free speech. It was about a student entering a confidential records area at the college." "NWTC welcomes and encourages free speech at all our campuses and locations," Smits said. "And in fact, as part of their education, our students are welcome to assemble and exercise free speech anywhere on campus that does not disrupt learning or enter areas that handle confidential records."

predictions.text: In 2018, Olsen, represented by the conservative-leaning Wisconsin Institute of Law & Liberty, filed a free speech lawsuit against Northeast Wisconsin Technical College. The case in the U.S. Eastern District of Wisconsin is ongoing.
predictions.reasoning: Contains the directional term 'Northeast' as part of a college name.

results.plain_text: A self-declared cougar has hit out at trolls who tell her older women can't be "sexy" as she claims to be proof women can be "desirable" at any age.  Alex, an interior designer and raunchy content creator from Aspen, Colorado, can often be seen flaunting her toned body in tiny bikinis on social media. But while the single 56-year-old, who describes herself as a "hot cougar", claims younger men are always flirting with her she revealed not everyone has the same response to her racy outfits.  Taking to TikTok, where she's known by the nickname Aspen Cougar, Alex said trolls have told her "You can't be sexy over 50". But in the video, she hit back at critics as she swayed around in a gold sequin ball gown that clung to her curves and featured a plunging neckline.  For more real life stories, click here.  Alex traced her hands down her cleavage before lifting her dress up to her thighs to show off the stiletto heels and stockings she was wearing underneath. She captioned the post: "Women can be hot and desirable at any age. Own your age!"  In another video shared to her Instagram, Alex revealed she's also been told by keyboard warriors that she's "too old to wear a bikini". But she defied their comments in the clip as she pranced around in a skimpy black cut-out thong bikini and matching black sun hat.  “I’m old enough to do as I please," she wrote alongside the post. When it comes to dealing with cruel comments, Alex revealed she usually just ignores trolls.  She told The Sun: "I tell them I have earned the right to do as I damn well please and then I block them." She also opened up about her cougar lifestyle, revealing she's dated men as young as 19.  Recalling how she started dating younger men, she said: "For the past several years, whenever I went out with my girlfriends, I was frequently getting hit on by men in their 20s and 30s, and rarely by men my age or older. It was extremely flattering and I embraced it."  Alex revealed younger men, who she calls "cubbies", are "eager learners" and she loves to share her "knowledge and experience" with them. While she claims she's not opposed to dating men her own age, Alex said she struggles to find any she's attracted to.  But she explained younger men are able to keep up with her stamina. "Many younger gentlemen have amazing energy, enthusiasm, and I love how complimentary they are towards me, they express gratitude for spending time with me. I’m a high-energy lady and enjoy how vibrant they are," she said.  Join the Daily Star's WhatsApp for the sexiest headlines, showbiz gossip and lots more  The Daily Star is now on WhatsApp and we want you to join us!  Through the app, we'll send you the sassiest showbiz stories, some naught headline and a seismic smattering of aliens...along with the latest breaking news of course.  To join our community, all you have to do to join is click on this link, select 'Join Chat' and you're in!  No one will be able to see who has sign up and no one can send messages except for the Daily Star team. We also treat our community members to competitions, special offers, promotions, and adverts from us and our partners.  If you don’t like our community, you can check out any time you like. To leave our community click on the name at the top of your screen and choose Exit group. If you’re curious, you can read our Privacy Notice.

results.actual_text: houses of origin to survive this year’s legislative session. House Finance Chairman Eric Nelson, R-Kanawha, said HB 2817 “ream
results.plain_text: By RYAN QUINN  Charleston Gazette-Mail  CHARLESTON, W.Va. — The West Virginia House of Delegates voted 76-24 Tuesday to pass on to the Senate a bill that eliminates the eight Regional Education Services Agencies, the state Office of Education Performance Audits and the mandate that public school year calendars have at least 180 separate instructional days.    It doesn’t include the $808 statewide teacher pay raise he originally proposed.  It maintains the requirement that the state School Building Authority consider “economies of scale” among the “objective criteria” for choosing which school construction and renovation projects to fund.  The House version also nixes the language in Justice’s version that says no single performance measure can compromise “a school or school system’s accreditation.”  The Legislature and the board have butted heads over which of them has authority over which facets of education. In recent years, the power struggle has centered on the board’s adoption of Common Core standards and the standardized tests aligned to them.  The House version also includes language that would seem to push the board to adopt the ACT — or perhaps the SAT, although that test is taken less frequently by West Virginia students — as the standardized test for 11th grade. The bill says the “board shall adopt a standard, curriculum-based achievement college entrance examination for grade eleven. The college entrance examination must include separate tests in English, reading, writing, mathematics and science.”  The House also added to Justice’s bill language requiring that the board provide “online assessment preparation” for students who must take online tests.  Before the bill got to the House floor, the GOP-controlled House Finance Committee moved the teacher pay raise to another bill. House Finance also removed — and didn’t move elsewhere — the school service personnel raise that the House Education Committee had added to Justice’s version. School service personnel include bus drivers, cooks and custodians.  Delegates put off action Tuesday on House Bill 2817, the bill the teacher pay raise was moved to. The House Rules Committee put it on the inactive calendar.  Today is “crossover day,” the deadline for when bills generally are supposed to be passed out of their houses of origin to survive this year’s legislative session.  House Finance Chairman Eric Nelson, R-Kanawha, said HB 2817 “reamortizes” the Teachers Retirement System unfunded liability, saving the state $70 million annually in debt payments over a 30-year period but increasing the total payment period by an additional eight years.  Justice spokesman Grant Herring said that, according to Jeffrey Fleck, executive director of the state’s Consolidated Public Retirement Board, the bill would cost taxpayers an extra $1.55 billion to fully pay down the debt.  “That is going to be the one funding mechanism for the teachers’ pay raise,” Nelson said of HB 2817. “Without that, I would not see any possibility of being able to fund the teacher pay raise in our budget right now.”  When asked after Tuesday morning’s House floor session why action on that bill had been delayed, he said, “I think it’s looking at some of the other items or bills that are on the agenda and just prioritizing.  “It’s just dropped to the bottom, and whether we bring that up or whether it dies is yet to be determined.”  See more from the Charleston Gazette-Mail

results.actual_text: TikTok user Nia Nicole shared an adorable moment with netizens as her unborn
results.plain_text: TikTok user Nia Nicole shared an adorable moment with netizens as her unborn baby girl flipped in her big belly She explained in the trending TikTok video her baby turned to face head down, and the clip caused an online buzz Some social media users said the experience would frighten them, while others expressed a desire to have babies Pregnancy is a sweet journey where a woman can begin to bond with her unborn child before giving birth. Do unborn babies move in belly? A pregnant woman has shared a video capturing the moment her unborn baby made some movements in her belly. The TikTok user Nia Nicole elicited mixed reactions from netizens after the clip went viral on social media. "To think I am going to meet her any day now," read the caption on the heartwarming video. What causes baby fever? Nicole was relaxing when recording herself, showing off her bulging pregnancy bump that gave women baby fevers. While recording, her unborn baby turned to face downward, leaving the mother-to-be stunned. "Oh my God, what is that?" she said as she kept her cool while the baby turned as seen in the video. Social media reactions The 10-second clip went viral, garnering comments from social media users who flocked to the comment section. While some expressed how they would react in such a moment, others noted they had experienced the same. Ameeyah Realtor: "I would have passed out ." Cosmic: "I remembered when my son moved head down very painful now he just kicks my ribs and is now mad at the eviction notice this Thursday." Jastiny: "I remember my experience too. I was laying there in shock for a minute. I did some techniques to get mine to flip. And she finally gave in that night. My midwife told me she was definitely head down." Tee’Lahh: "I know that hurts because my baby has been hurting me, moving around and she is not even that big yet." Atienoh Okoth: "My husband was rubbing my tummy when he felt the baby's kick and let me tell you Maina...he screamed like hell never to touch my tummy again." Can pregnant woman have flat tummy? Separately, a pregnant woman showed off her tiny belly in a stunning video. The expectant mother revealed she was seven months pregnant, but her baby bump was barely showing. In the video shared via the TikTok app, she showed how she was still physically agile despite being in her third trimester. PAY ATTENTION: check out news exactly for YOU ➡️ click on “Recommended for you” and enjoy! Source: TUKO.co.ke

results.actual_text: Tunisians used to say that one in five of their countrymen worked for
results.plain_text: Tunisians used to say that one in five of their countrymen worked for the secret police. Leyla Bouzid was an aspiring young noughties film-maker in Tunis’s Fédération des Cinéastes Amateurs when she found out that one of her close friends in the club was an informer. It wasn’t an isolated experience. Of the paranoia that runs through her galloping debut feature As I Open My Eyes, about a rock group trying to find their voice and hold their nerve in the final months of former president Ben Ali’s rule, she says plainly: “These are things either that I saw, or that friends or myself lived through.”  Bouzid left Tunisia shortly after the informer incident to study literature at the Sorbonne, but returned as soon as she could, in fiction. As I Open My Eyes pivots around an incandescent performance from Baya Medhaffar as the corkscrew-haired chanteuse Farah, foolhardy and fiery in her determination to confront the regime. People’s Choice award-winner at Venice last year, it is a fine addition to a strong recent crop of feminist Middle Eastern and Maghreb cinema, including Wadjda, Mustang, the Moroccan prostitution drama Much Loved, and the Pakistani thriller Dukhtar. But for Bouzid, the film’s importance is precisely Tunisian; its mission to return to 2010 and the stirrings of what became the Arab spring. “It was very important for me to go back,” she says. “It’s true there was this revolution, and the world went: ‘Wow!’ But we didn’t go deeply into things at all. People took to the streets, but the problems were much more profound.”  As I Open My Eyes intimately explores the effects of living in a police state; both the all-pervasive fear and the self-censorship it produces in society at large. Farah’s mother, who tries to protect her by insisting she stop her rebellion, is her most visible enemy for most of the film. Ben Ali may be long gone, but such problems have not vanished: Bouzid cites the 2014 arrest of blogger Azyz Amami under law 52, an anti-drug measure still used by the Tunisian state to clamp down on youth expression. She is in no doubt that there is major work to be done if the country is to move forward. “I believe in the necessity of a cultural revolution in our country. Not just political revolution, but creating new forms of thought.”  Holding court the morning after her film has screened at Cinemed festival in Montpellier, the 32-year-old is getting into her stride. “Our young musicians and artists don’t have a voice in Europe today. We just talk about Daesh etc. [But] in times when there’s been a very, very strong kind of extremism, like during the wars of religion in the west, that’s given rise to a new thought. A rejection of extremism.” She tails off, almost wistfully. “It’s very personal. Maybe it’s just me that thinks that.”  She cast Medhaffar in the film, made for just €600,000 (£500,000), exactly because she seemed uninhibited by the past and ready to embrace new things such as a degree of sensuality unusual in films from the region (Bouzid worked as assistant director on fellow Tunisian Abdellatif Kechiche’s Blue Is the Warmest Colour). “She’s very free in her head. She was one of the few people who didn’t say: I can’t do that because my mother and father wouldn’t like it.” There is, Bouzid admits, some correlation between herself and Farah. “I was also very free in my head, but I was smarter than Farah. I was aware of what I didn’t have the right to do, but I knew how to get around it.” She partly means romance – Farah’s second preoccupation in the film.  “Love affairs when you’re 18 in Tunis aren’t simple. Especially if you’re headstrong by nature. But it’s very Mediterranean, too; you see that idea in southern France or Italy. A girl with a thirst for life who doesn’t want limits. And a boy who really loves her, but can’t control her and gets possessive.”  Bouzid grew up with her mother, a GP and culture buff who introduced her to Citizen Kane and Bergman: ironically, although her father is Nouri Bouzid, perhaps Tunisia’s leading postwar director, he never took her to the cinema (her parents are divorced). Distancing herself from his reputation was another reason she decided to study in France.  “I’ve got a good rapport with my father, but I try to progress on my own, a way off from him. He read a version of the script, but I didn’t let him come on set. My production company has never worked with him. That was important for me: he’s someone with strong opinions; you can’t take it for granted that you’re going to form your own.” They both make socially conscious films – her father’s 1986 debut Man of Ashes dealt with homosexuality in Tunisia, and 1989’s Golden Horseshoes with the failure of the country’s May 1968 protesters – but in different ways. “He said I relate violent things with a caress, but he does it with claws,” says Bouzid.  Actually, As I Open My Eyes has both at its disposal: in musical terms, both the crepuscular laments narcotically delivered by Farah, and punky rai moshpit-botherers. All were purpose-written by Iraqi-British oud master Khyam Allami to fit lyrics by Bouzid’s poet friend Ghassen Amami. Small gestures, but hopefully resonant ones in keeping with the director’s idea of a DIY cultural revolution that might actually take hold in the scorched earth that has killed the Arab spring. “There’s been a long wait for cinema for young people. There’s a Tunisian audience, but also in other Arab countries, waiting for this kind of film that gives voice to this kind of energetic youth.”  • As I Open My Eyes is showing at Safar film festival of contemporary Arab cinema on 17 September. The festival runs from 14 to 18 September.  • This article was amended on 3 September 2016. The original stated Khyam Allami was Syrian-British. This has been corrected.
